metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: AC6 claims release evidence 'records every action SHA, the Python build-tool versions, the MCP publisher version + verified digest', but the proving command only checks that the three fragment names and a consolidation step appear in `release.yml` YAML — it never asserts the CONTENT of the uploaded artifacts contains the required data. An implementation could upload empty or incomplete evidence files and pass all static tests.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (release evidence); Scope — test_release_workflow_pins.py check (f)

findings.finding: The plan splits the existing mcp_registry job (release.yml lines 109-179, currently 'needs: publish') into two jobs (mcp_verify + mcp_registry), but never specifies what mcp_verify's 'needs:' dependency is, nor which of the two new jobs inherits the 'Wait for PyPI' polling step currently in mcp_registry — leaving the job-chain topology underspecified.
findings.location: What section (MCP publisher — download → verify → execute) / Scope (.github/workflows/release.yml split)
findings.suggested_fix: Assign 'Wait for PyPI' to mcp_verify (no OIDC token needed) and set 'needs: publish' on mcp_verify, with mcp_registry declaring 'needs: [mcp_verify]'. This preserves the PyPI-readiness guard before OIDC scope is activated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the plan explicitly requires `mcp_verify` to declare `needs: publish` (or an equivalent predecessor) so the `mcp_verify` → `mcp_registry` chain still runs after PyPI publishing completes — enumerate this as a named constraint in the Scope edit to `release.yml`.
findings.finding: The plan splits the existing `mcp_registry` job (which has `needs: publish` at `release.yml:111`) into `mcp_verify` + `mcp_registry`, but never specifies that `mcp_verify` must inherit `needs: publish`. Without this, `mcp_verify` could run concurrently with (or before) the PyPI publish job, breaking the sequencing contract that ensures the MCP registry is only updated after PyPI has the new version.
findings.location: Scope section — `release.yml`; `What` section — MCP publisher job split; existing `release.yml:111` `needs: publish`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `mcp_verify` job specification in the What/Scope sections: `needs: publish` (in addition to any internal `mcp_verify`→`mcp_registry` chaining), preserving the existing sequencing contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `mcp_verify` (or an intermediate job) carries `needs: publish` so the post-PyPI ordering invariant is preserved after the job split, and assign explicit ownership of the 'Wait for PyPI' polling step to one of the two new jobs.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `mcp_registry needs: mcp_verify` but never specifies what `mcp_verify` itself depends on. The current `mcp_registry` job has `needs: publish` (release.yml line 111), ensuring it runs only after PyPI has accepted the release. If `mcp_verify` has no `needs: publish`, it will execute concurrently with (or before) `publish`, and the 'Wait for PyPI to serve this version' polling step — which must run before `mcp-publisher publish` — has no assigned home in the new two-job structure. The mechanism that currently serializes the MCP registration behind PyPI publication is silently dropped.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; Scope — 'mcp_verify … mcp_registry'; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add `needs: [publish]` to `mcp_verify` (or `needs: [publish, mcp_verify]` to `mcp_registry`), and retain the 'Wait for PyPI' polling loop in `mcp_registry` (since it only matters immediately before `mcp-publisher publish`).

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'final aggregation step' that downloads all evidence fragments and concatenates them into `release-evidence.txt`, but does not name which job hosts this step or what its `needs:` dependencies and permissions should be. An executor must invent this design choice without guidance.
findings.location: What section — 'Print and retain release evidence' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet (release evidence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'A new `release_evidence` job (`needs: [build_dist, mcp_verify, mcp_registry]`, no elevated permissions) runs `actions/download-artifact` for each fragment and concatenates them: `cat evidence-shapin/tool-versions.txt evidence-build/build-info.txt evidence-mcp/mcp-info.txt > release-evidence.txt`, then uploads the consolidated artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the pip-tools capability gap: either (a) add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` and confirm the docs AC grep check matches the chosen tool, or (b) adopt `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (uv is already present in the repo's toolchain) and update the Scope/AC/docs grep check to match; in either case, specify how the lock file will be kept current (e.g., extend `dependabot.yml` with a `pip` ecosystem block, or document a manual refresh cadence in the Pinning section).
findings.finding: `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) is mandated as the canonical lock-file generation command in the Scope, AC3, and the docs AC grep check (a literal `grep -q 'pip-compile --generate-hashes'`), but `pip-tools` is absent from `pyproject.toml [dev]`, absent from every workflow, and the repo's `dependabot.yml` explicitly excludes the `pip` ecosystem — so the capability assertion is unverified and there is no mechanism to keep the lock file current.
findings.location: Scope — `.github/release-requirements.txt`; AC3; docs AC (grep check for `pip-compile --generate-hashes`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pip-tools` to `[project.optional-dependencies] dev` in `pyproject.toml`, or switch to `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (consistent with the `uv` already referenced in the plan) and update all AC grep checks accordingly. Add a `pip` ecosystem entry to `.github/dependabot.yml` scoped to `.github/release-requirements.txt` to surface stale pins automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add pip-tools to the repo's dev/release toolchain (e.g., a `[project.optional-dependencies]` entry, a Makefile target `make release-lock`, or a documented `pip install pip-tools` step in `docs/releasing.md`) so the `pip-compile --generate-hashes` command is discoverable and runnable without prior knowledge — and confirm whether `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (the plan's own stated alternative) can satisfy the docs AC grep check, or remove it as a stated alternative.
findings.finding: pip-tools (`pip-compile --generate-hashes`) is introduced as a new external tool integration — the canonical lock-file generation command the plan commits to in Scope, AC3, and the docs AC grep check — but it is absent from pyproject.toml [dev] dependencies, absent from every workflow, and absent from the Makefile. No installation path is specified for a developer who needs to regenerate `.github/release-requirements.txt`. The docs AC additionally hard-couples to the literal substring `pip-compile --generate-hashes` in `docs/releasing.md`, meaning the `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` alternative mentioned in the What section cannot satisfy the grep check. If pip-tools is not installed in the reviewer's or CI environment, the lock-file regeneration step has no discoverable entry point.
findings.location: Scope section (.github/release-requirements.txt bullet), AC3, docs AC (docs/releasing.md grep check for 'pip-compile --generate-hashes')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `make release-lock` Makefile target that runs `pip install pip-tools && pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in`, and add `pip-tools` to the docs/releasing.md 'Pinning' section's update instructions (which the docs AC already requires to document the pin-update process). This simultaneously satisfies AC7 (docs) and the T3 integration gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pip-tools` as a named edit target — either to `pyproject.toml [dev]` dependencies or as a documented `pipx install pip-tools` prerequisite in `docs/releasing.md`'s Pinning section — so the lock-file regeneration command `pip-compile --generate-hashes` is executable without out-of-band tooling discovery.
findings.finding: `pip-compile` (from `pip-tools`) is named as the canonical lock-file generation command throughout the plan (Scope, AC3, docs AC) but `pip-tools` is not listed as a dev dependency in `pyproject.toml`, not installed in any existing CI workflow, and the plan does not name it as an edit target (neither adding it to `pyproject.toml [dev]`, nor to a CI install step, nor to a `pipx`/standalone install instruction). An implementer cannot run `pip-compile --generate-hashes` to regenerate `.github/release-requirements.txt` without a separately-sourced tool.
findings.location: Scope section — `.github/release-requirements.txt`; AC3; docs AC (pip-compile substring check)
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml`'s `dev` extra (consistent with how `git-cliff` is documented as a `pipx install` prerequisite in the Makefile), or add an explicit `pipx install pip-tools==<pinned-version>` install instruction to the docs/releasing.md Pinning section alongside the `pip-compile` regeneration command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the `(or `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`)` parenthetical from the `What` section so it matches the committed choice in Scope and ACs — state one canonical tool.
findings.finding: The `What` section presents `pip-compile --generate-hashes (pip-tools)` and `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as co-equal alternatives ('or'), but the Scope section and all ACs commit exclusively to `pip-compile`. An executor reading the `What` section first could choose `uv pip compile`, produce a `uv`-formatted lock file, and then fail the AC grep for `pip-compile --generate-hashes` in `docs/releasing.md` — or diverge from the Scope's regeneration command.
findings.location: What section — 'Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file … `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) (or `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the `What` bullet to read: '…generated and maintained with `pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in` (pip-tools), checked into the repo.' — drop the `uv` alternative entirely from this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `persist-credentials: false` caveat in the What section with the absolute AC: either (a) remove the 'unless' clause from What and make the rule absolute (aligning with the AC and the test), or (b) add the same caveat to the AC and specify how `test_release_workflow_pins.py` should handle the allowed exception.
findings.finding: The What section qualifies `persist-credentials: false` with 'unless a later git operation demonstrably needs the persisted credential', but the AC states absolutely 'Every release checkout sets `persist-credentials: false`' with no such caveat. An implementer who encounters a checkout that genuinely needs a persisted credential would face contradictory directives from two authoritative sections.
findings.location: What section ('Set `persist-credentials: false` on every release checkout unless a later git operation demonstrably needs the persisted credential') vs AC line 2 ('Every release checkout sets `persist-credentials: false`')
findings.suggested_fix: If no checkout in `release.yml` actually needs a persisted credential (which the Why section implies), remove the 'unless a later git operation demonstrably needs the persisted credential' clause from the What section to match the absolute AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the evidence-aggregation step/job to the Scope section's `release.yml` bullet so that the Scope section is a complete enumeration of changes to that file.
findings.finding: The final aggregation step — which downloads all evidence fragments and concatenates them into one `release-evidence.txt` — is described in the What section and mandated by AC 6, but is not listed as a deliverable in the Scope section. The Scope section enumerates changes to `release.yml`, new files, and new tests, but omits the aggregation job/step as a named change to `release.yml`.
findings.location: Scope section vs What section ('a final aggregation step downloads all fragments and concatenates them into one `release-evidence.txt`') and AC 6 ('a final aggregation step downloads all fragments and concatenates them into one `release-evidence.txt`')
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the `.github/workflows/release.yml` bullet in Scope: 'add a final evidence-aggregation job that uses `download-artifact` for all three fragment names and concatenates them into `release-evidence.txt`.'

findings.finding: The 'What' section permits an exception to persist-credentials: false ('unless a later git operation demonstrably needs the persisted credential'), but AC #2 and its test have no exception mechanism — the test unconditionally fails if any checkout lacks the flag. An implementer who legitimately needs persisted credentials for a git push would find the plan self-contradictory: the body authorizes an exception, the AC forbids it.
findings.location: What section ('Set persist-credentials: false … unless a later git operation …') vs. AC #2 ('checks every actions/checkout block … and fails if it is absent')
findings.suggested_fix: If no step in release.yml actually requires persisted credentials, remove the 'unless' clause from the What section so the AC's unconditional assertion is the sole authoritative rule. If a step does need it, document which checkout that is and add a named exception to both the What description and the test logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test assertion (e.g. check (h)) to `test_release_workflow_pins.py` that verifies the pip install step in `release.yml` contains both `--require-hashes` and `--no-deps`, so AC3's hash-locked install guarantee is statically enforced alongside the `--no-isolation` check.
findings.finding: The test assertions enumerated in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` ((a) through (g)) do not include verifying that the pip install step in `release.yml` contains `--require-hashes --no-deps`. AC3 states that hash-locked install IS the verification mechanism (pip fails the build if a hash mismatches), but if `--require-hashes` is accidentally omitted from the workflow the static test (which only asserts `--no-isolation` per check (c)) would still pass, leaving the hash-locked install AC silently unverified.
findings.location: AC3 ('Release build tools install from `.github/release-requirements.txt` … with `--require-hashes`') and `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` assertions (a)-(g)
findings.suggested_fix: In the test-assertions list, append: '(h) the pip install step targeting `.github/release-requirements.txt` includes both `--require-hashes` and `--no-deps` flags (fail if either is absent).' Mirror this in AC3's verification sentence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 3) to assert that `.github/release-requirements.in` exists in the repository and lists at minimum `build`, `hatchling`, and `twine` as direct dependencies.
findings.finding: `.github/release-requirements.in` is introduced as a new deliverable in the Scope section (the human-maintained source of truth for the lock file), but no AC verifies its existence, content, or that it is checked into the repository. The ACs cover only the compiled output `release-requirements.txt`.
findings.location: Scope section ('`.github/release-requirements.txt` (new, the hash-locked constraints file) compiled from `.github/release-requirements.in`') vs AC 3 (covers only `release-requirements.txt`)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 3: 'The `.github/release-requirements.in` file is checked into the repo and lists at minimum `build`, `hatchling`, and `twine`; `test_release_workflow_pins.py` may assert the file exists or the presence assertion can be a documentation requirement in `docs/releasing.md`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `docs/releasing.md` 'Pinning' section (or add a subsection) to document (a) the `--no-isolation` rationale (why the default isolated build would bypass the hash-locked env) and (b) the `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` job-split security contract (why step ordering within one job does not prevent OIDC token exposure), and update the corresponding AC substrings so `test_release_workflow_pins.py` verifies both are present.
findings.finding: The `--no-isolation` build strategy (a deliberate deviation from PEP 517 isolated builds) and the `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` job-split security architecture are novel, consequential decisions — but the `docs/releasing.md` AC only mandates three pinning-related substrings (`pip-compile --generate-hashes`, `MCP publisher`, `full-SHA`). Neither the `--no-isolation` rationale nor the job-split security model is required to appear in any durable doc (releasing.md, ADR, or CLAUDE.md), so a future maintainer who removes `--no-isolation` or collapses the jobs has no written record of why those choices were made.
findings.location: Scope → `docs/releasing.md`; AC line 'docs/releasing.md contains a "Pinning" section covering, at minimum…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add two required substrings to the docs AC — e.g., `--no-isolation` and `id-token isolation` (or equivalent) — and add one sentence each to the releasing.md Pinning section explaining the rationale. Alternatively, add a CLAUDE.md / ADR entry for the job-split security model so the rationale survives even if releasing.md is restructured.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to Scope (and a brief docs/releasing.md note) a concrete pin-update mechanism — either enabling Dependabot for GitHub Actions (`dependabot.yml` with `package-ecosystem: github-actions`) and a manual process for the MCP publisher SHA, or a scheduled workflow that checks the MCP publisher releases feed and opens a PR — so pin freshness is maintained without relying solely on manual vigilance.
findings.finding: The hardcoded MCP publisher SHA-256 (and exact-version URL) and all action commit SHAs are values that must be updated whenever the MCP publisher or an action releases a new version — but the plan specifies no automated mechanism (e.g., Dependabot for GitHub Actions, Renovate, or a scheduled workflow) to surface staleness. The plan says 'Update pins through reviewed dependency-update changes' but names no tooling, leaving the update process entirely manual and easy to neglect.
findings.location: What → MCP publisher section ('Update pins through reviewed dependency-update changes, not implicit resolution during a production release'); Scope → `.github/release-requirements.txt`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `.github/dependabot.yml` entry for `package-ecosystem: github-actions` (directory `/`) to automate action-SHA updates, and document in releasing.md that the MCP publisher SHA must be manually re-pinned when a new version is adopted (with the exact curl+sha256sum commands to generate the new value). This makes the manual scope explicit and bounded rather than implicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry behavior for the `curl` download in `mcp_verify` (e.g., `curl --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 --retry-all-errors -fsSL …`) so transient network errors do not permanently fail a release run.
findings.finding: The `mcp_verify` job's `curl -fsSL` download of the MCP publisher binary has no retry or backoff. A single transient CDN or GitHub Releases network error will fail the entire release pipeline with no recovery path short of a manual re-run, which is operationally painful during a time-sensitive release.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher steps; `mcp_verify` job specification
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz` with `curl --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 --retry-all-errors -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`. Document this in the AC and in the `mcp_verify` job specification. The sha256sum check that follows already ensures a partially-downloaded or corrupt file is rejected regardless of retries.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` so `make install` provides `pip-compile`, OR change the docs AC grep string to a tool-agnostic invariant (e.g. `--generate-hashes`) and document the choice of tool in the Pinning section without hardcoding it into a grep contract.
findings.finding: pip-tools (`pip-compile`) is introduced as a brand-new developer tool dependency that is absent from `pyproject.toml [dev]`, absent from every workflow, and absent from the Makefile — yet the docs AC hard-codes the literal substring `pip-compile --generate-hashes` as the single required grep check, baking in the tool identity as a testable contract. The plan itself acknowledges `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` is equivalent (parenthetical in 'What'); locking the docs grep to pip-tools over uv (or vice-versa) is premature tool-choice proliferation when neither is currently present in the dev stack.
findings.location: Scope section (.github/release-requirements.txt paragraph) and AC #3 and AC #7 (docs grep check)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the docs AC grep target from `pip-compile --generate-hashes` to `--generate-hashes` (tool-agnostic), document both pip-tools and uv as supported options in `docs/releasing.md`, and either add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` or leave it as an external tool with an explicit install note — but do not embed the tool name as a testable contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `sha256sum -c` step for the extracted `mcp-publisher` binary in `mcp_registry` after `download-artifact`, so the privileged job independently verifies the binary's digest before execution, completing the stated security contract.
findings.finding: The plan's security contract states 'the OIDC-privileged job never downloads or holds an unverified binary', but the sha256sum check runs in `mcp_verify` on the *archive* (`.tar.gz`), not on the *extracted binary*. The `mcp_registry` job downloads the extracted binary from GHA artifact storage via `download-artifact` and executes it without any re-verification of the binary's digest. The claimed security property — that `mcp_registry` never executes an unverified binary — is not fully enforced by the mechanism as specified.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute'; 'Security contract — structural job split'; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In `mcp_registry`, after `actions/download-artifact`, add: `echo "<same-pinned-sha256-of-binary>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` before `chmod +x`. The binary SHA-256 can be derived by hashing the known-good extracted binary during pin time, alongside the archive SHA-256.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to the leaf plan acknowledging the parent's governance obligation: plan-review advisory findings must be recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale as a comment on ticket `08a8-b04e-ae59-4be4`, and every Accepted finding must be reflected in the description before the change receives its Gerrit `LLM-Review +1` / `Verified +1` votes.
findings.finding: The parent epic's Acceptance Criteria imposes a governance obligation on every child story — 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale (as a comment on the child ticket), and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before that child's change receives its Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes' — but this leaf's plan contains no mention of this requirement in its ACs, scope, or anywhere else. The leaf is therefore silent on a deliverable the parent explicitly requires of it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all ACs); parent ticket 2610-9557-c3c1-460c AC: 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add as the final AC: '[ ] All plan-review advisory findings for this story are recorded on ticket `08a8-b04e-ae59-4be4` with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale; every Accepted finding is reflected in this description before the Gerrit `LLM-Review +1` / `Verified +1` votes are cast (parent epic AC).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit comparison of `[build-system].requires = ["hatchling==X.Y.Z"]` vs `--no-isolation` + hash-locked env in the plan's 'Hatchling enforcement — decision' sub-section, documenting the specific reason the established PEP 517 mechanism is not used (or, if it is sufficient, adopt it instead).
findings.finding: The plan adopts `python -m build --no-isolation` (a deliberate PEP 517 deviation) to enforce Hatchling's version through the pre-installed hash-locked env, but does not evaluate the established alternative — pinning Hatchling in `[build-system].requires` — before committing to this novel approach.
findings.location: What — 'Hatchling enforcement — decision'; Scope — `pyproject.toml — no change`; AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `zizmor` or `actionlint` was evaluated and rejected for the SHA-pin / `persist-credentials` checks (ACs 1–2); if neither was considered, add a brief rationale in the plan for why bespoke grep is preferred (e.g., 'no new binary dep', 'already have the YAML-grep pattern in test_ci_workflow_parity.py').
findings.finding: The plan mandates two new bespoke Python test files (`test_release_workflow_pins.py`, `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py`) that perform raw YAML text-search / grep-style enforcement on `release.yml` for SHA-pin compliance (AC1), `persist-credentials` (AC2), and `--no-isolation` (AC4). This rebuilds functionality that established GitHub Actions security linters (`zizmor`, `actionlint`) already provide for the SHA-pin and `persist-credentials` checks specifically — without acknowledging or rejecting those tools. The codebase has no existing use of either tool (confirmed by search), but the plan provides no rationale for the NIH choice.
findings.location: Scope section (tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py), AC #1, AC #2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for choosing bespoke YAML-grep in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` over `zizmor`/`actionlint` for action-pin and persist-credentials enforcement (ACs 1 and 2), or consider delegating those two specific checks to `zizmor` (callable from Python via `subprocess` within the existing test) and reserving the bespoke grep for checks zizmor cannot cover (--no-isolation in run: scripts, MCP job permission structure, evidence fragment names).
findings.finding: The plan mandates bespoke YAML-grep logic in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` to enforce full-SHA `uses:`, `persist-credentials: false`, and `--no-isolation` without providing positive rationale for this choice over established, purpose-built tools (`zizmor` — a dedicated GHA supply-chain security linter that natively detects unpinned action refs and missing persist-credentials at the structural YAML level; `actionlint` — a CI linter with similar action-pin detection). For ACs 1 and 2 specifically, these tools provide structural YAML analysis rather than regex/grep, are actively maintained with security-specific rules, and are less fragile against YAML formatting variations. The plan cites the Gerrit/make-test integration as a reason for a Python test, but this rationale justifies the *test runner location* (Python/pytest vs. a separate workflow) not the choice of raw grep over a purpose-built linter as the detection engine.
findings.location: Scope — 'tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py'; AC1; AC2; What section — 'Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate `zizmor` and `actionlint` as prior art for SHA-pin and `persist-credentials` enforcement before committing to bespoke YAML-grep logic in `test_release_workflow_pins.py`; document the evaluation and rationale (e.g., why grep tests are preferred over an established linter invocation) in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan mandates bespoke YAML-grep/parse logic in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` (enforcing SHA-pins, `persist-credentials: false`, and `--no-isolation`) rather than adopting `zizmor` or `actionlint` — established, dedicated GitHub Actions security linters that already check unpinned action references and credential-persistence — without any stated evaluation or rejection rationale for these tools.
findings.location: Scope — `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` (new); What — 'a new, self-contained SHA-pin lint'; AC1, AC2, AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-section to the 'What' or Scope: 'Prior art evaluated: `zizmor` (checks unpinned actions + credential-persistence; Python-installable) and `actionlint` (static linter) were considered. We use bespoke pytest assertions because [reason — e.g., zizmor's output is not deterministic enough for our Gerrit gate / it cannot assert --no-isolation / it is not yet in pyproject.toml dev deps].' If the evaluation reveals no blocking reason, adopt `zizmor --format sarif` as the SHA-pin/credential check and reserve the pytest file for repo-specific checks (`--no-isolation`, job-permission structure) that zizmor cannot assert.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `permissions:` block — either at workflow level (`permissions: read-all` or equivalent) and/or per-job — for ALL jobs in `release.yml`, not just `mcp_registry`; the AC and test assertions for `test_release_workflow_pins.py` should verify that no job holds permissions beyond its declared minimum (aligns with the GitHub Security hardening guidance the plan already cites).
findings.finding: The plan declares the permission structure for mcp_verify (no id-token: write) and mcp_registry (id-token: write) but does NOT specify a workflow-level default permissions block or per-job permissions for the remaining jobs (build, aggregation/consolidation). The plan itself cites GitHub's Security hardening for GitHub Actions guidance; that same guidance recommends setting permissions: read-all at the workflow level and granting elevated permissions only per-job. Without an explicit workflow-level permissions: declaration, all jobs (including the build and evidence-aggregation jobs) inherit the repository's Actions default, which may be read-and-write — meaning the build job runs with unnecessary contents: write or packages: write grants even though it only needs read access. This is an undeclared access posture for the non-publishing jobs on a workflow that holds OIDC credentials in a sibling job.
findings.location: What / Scope / AC — workflow job permissions (build and aggregation jobs not covered); plan references GitHub Security hardening guidance
findings.suggested_fix: Add `permissions: read-all` at the top of `release.yml` (workflow-level default), then override per job: `mcp_registry` gets `id-token: write` + `contents: read` only; the build job and aggregation job need at most `contents: read`. Assert in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` that a workflow-level or per-job permissions block exists and that no non-publishing job declares `id-token: write` or `contents: write`.

findings.finding: The `test_release_workflow_pins.py` static checks do not assert that `chmod +x mcp-publisher` is present in the `mcp_registry` job, yet the plan itself identifies its absence as a runtime 'Permission denied' failure. The AC states 'mcp_registry must chmod +x mcp-publisher after download-artifact' but no proving command covers this — an implementer who omits the step would pass all tests and hit a silent runtime breakage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (MCP publisher split jobs); What section — 'Restore the executable bit after the handoff'
findings.suggested_fix: In the test's job-permission-structure check (assertion (e)), also parse the `mcp_registry` job's steps list and assert that at least one step's `run` field matches `chmod +x mcp-publisher` and that it appears after the download-artifact step. Example: `assert any('chmod +x mcp-publisher' in (s.get('run') or '') for s in mcp_registry_steps), 'mcp_registry missing chmod +x mcp-publisher'`.

findings.finding: The plan explicitly identifies 'chmod +x mcp-publisher after download-artifact' as a must-have runtime correctness requirement ("or the ./mcp-publisher login call fails 'Permission denied'") but omits a static assertion for it from test_release_workflow_pins.py's enumerated check list (checks a–f), leaving this failure mode undetectable under `make test`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — MCP publisher AC bullet (chmod +x callout) and Scope — tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py check list (a–f)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` spec, append check (g): after locating the download-artifact step in mcp_registry, assert the next step (or any subsequent step before the first `./mcp-publisher` invocation) contains `chmod +x mcp-publisher`. Mirror the pattern already used for checks (b) and (e) which inspect job-level YAML structure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update `docs/releasing.md` line 45's `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` reference to the new SHA pin as part of this story's docs edit, or explicitly acknowledge in the plan that the Supply-chain attestations section's quoted tag will become stale and record it as accepted debt with a rationale.
findings.finding: The plan scopes docs/releasing.md changes to the new 'Pinning' section only, explicitly excluding the existing 'Supply-chain attestations' section — but that section (line 45) currently quotes the mutable tag `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` verbatim. After this story SHA-pins that action in release.yml, the inline doc reference becomes factually stale. The plan neither updates the quoted string in the excluded section nor flags this as a known accepted inconsistency, leaving a false codebase premise embedded in the documentation.
findings.location: Scope section ('Section ownership: this story owns the "Pinning" section only'); docs/releasing.md line 45
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the docs/releasing.md scope to include updating the quoted action reference on line 45 to the new full SHA (with version comment), so the 'Supply-chain attestations' section stays consistent with the pinned workflow.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
